core.intro: Across Australia passionfruit is grown much the same way: a vigorous vine in full sun on a strong support, fed and watered well through the warm months, and replaced every few years as it tires. What changes from state to state is the climate, the districts where it thrives, the harvest timing and the pests, which the state sections below cover. The notes here apply wherever you garden.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Passionfruit fall into two broad groups. The purple and black types (Passiflora edulis), including named selections such as Misty Gem, Sweetheart and Pandora and the grafted Nellie Kelly, carry the rich, sweet-tart flavour most Australians know and cope better with cooler conditions. The Panama and golden types (the larger, flavicarpa-influenced vines such as Panama Red and Panama Gold) bear bigger, milder fruit and take tropical heat and humidity better. Choose the purple types for flavour and cooler gardens, the Panama types for size and warm or humid districts. Sweet granadilla and giant granadilla are different Passiflora species again, milder and grown more as a curiosity. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties for sale right now.

sections.heading: Pollination: will one vine set fruit?
sections.body: Most purple and black passionfruit sold in Australia are self-fertile hybrids, so a single healthy vine sets a good crop on its own. The Panama and golden types are more often self-incompatible and set far better with a second, genetically different vine nearby to cross-pollinate, which is why a lone golden vine can flower heavily yet carry almost no fruit. Bees do the work: large carpenter bees are the most efficient pollinators and honeybees are effective too, so anything that keeps pollinators busy helps. If the flowers keep dropping without setting, pollinate by hand, brushing pollen from one flower onto the centre of another in the late morning.

sections.heading: Planting and support
sections.body: Passionfruit is a fast, heavy climber that needs a strong, permanent support: a sturdy fence, trellis or wire frame, not a flimsy lattice. Give it full sun, shelter from hard frost and strong wind, and a free-draining, slightly acidic soil with a pH of about 5.5 to 6.5. On heavy ground plant on a low mound, because the roots rot in wet soil. Work compost and a little dolomite into the hole, keep the root zone cool and mulched, and water the young vine in well. In cooler districts spring is the best planting time so the vine establishes before winter (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Passionfruit is a hungry, thirsty vine. Keep the soil consistently moist but never waterlogged, and never let it dry out while the vine is flowering and carrying fruit, because water stress then drops the crop; a mature vine can draw up to 140 litres a week at the height of summer. It is sensitive to salty water and salty soil, so use the best water you have and mulch heavily to hold moisture. Feed generously through the warm season, because potassium is the key nutrient for fruiting: use a balanced fertiliser with potassium at least equal to nitrogen (the Northern Territory growing note suggests about 200 grams of a 10:3:10 N:P:K blend per bearing vine each month, plus a little boron every few months to help fruit set). Poultry manure, compost and a potassium-rich fruit fertiliser all suit it. Go easy on high-nitrogen feeds, which push leafy growth at the expense of flowers and fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest and eating
sections.body: Passionfruit tells you when it is ready. The purple and black types ripen and fall to the ground, where you gather them, twice a day in hot weather so the sun does not scorch them; the Panama and golden types are usually picked from the vine once they have coloured fully. The fruit will not ripen much further once picked green, but a ripe one sweetens and its skin wrinkles as it sits, which concentrates the flavour. Scoop the pulp straight from the shell, or freeze it whole or as pulp when the vine is in full swing and you have more than you can eat.

sections.heading: Woodiness virus and a short vine life
sections.body: Passionfruit vines are not long-lived, and the main reason is passionfruit woodiness virus. This aphid-spread virus has no cure: it crinkles and mottles the leaves and turns the fruit small, hard and thick-skinned with little pulp. Once a vine shows those signs, pull it out rather than nurse it, and start again with fresh, virus-free stock. Even healthy vines tire, so commercial growers replant purple varieties every two to three years and tropical Panama vines sooner, while a backyard vine in a mild spot with low disease pressure may keep cropping for several years. Either way, plan to replace the vine every few years rather than expecting a lifetime from it.

sections.heading: Buying tips: grafted or seedling
sections.body: Purple and black passionfruit are often sold grafted, because on their own roots they are prone to fusarium wilt and other soil diseases; the vine is worked onto a hardy golden-passionfruit rootstock that resists those soil problems and adds vigour. Grafted vines are tougher, but watch the base: any shoots that appear below the graft are rootstock and must be rubbed off, or they overtake the vine and leave you with poor fruit. Panama and golden types are usually grown from seed and come reasonably true. Whichever you buy, choose healthy, named stock with no crinkled leaves, and check the in-stock table above for what is available now.

faqs.q: How much does a passionfruit vine cost?
faqs.a: Most passionfruit vines sell for somewhere between about 10 and 40 dollars, with common seedling-grown purple and Panama vines at the cheaper end and named, grafted vines and the large granadillas dearer. The in-stock table above lists the current prices across the nurseries we track, so you can compare before you buy.

faqs.q: Can I grow passionfruit in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, a passionfruit will grow in a large pot for a few years, which suits a courtyard or a sunny balcony. Use the biggest container you can, give it a strong trellis to climb, and keep the water and feeding up because pots dry out and run short of nutrients fast. A potted vine crops a little less and tires sooner than one in the ground, so expect to replace it more often.

faqs.q: How big does a passionfruit vine get, and how do I prune it?
faqs.a: A vigorous passionfruit can put on five metres or more of growth in a season and will quickly cover a fence or pergola, so it needs room and a strong frame. Prune in spring once frost has passed, thinning out tangled and dead wood and shortening long canes back to a framework; this lets light in and keeps the vine productive. Avoid pruning hard in autumn, because the soft regrowth is frost-tender.

faqs.q: Is banana passionfruit the same thing, and is it a problem?
faqs.a: No. Banana passionfruit (Passiflora tarminiana, often sold as P. mollissima) is a different species with long, soft, yellow fruit, and it is a recognised environmental weed in the cooler states, smothering native bush where birds spread its seed. It is not a declared noxious plant, but if you live near bushland it is better avoided in favour of the common purple or Panama passionfruit.

faqs.q: Can I grow passionfruit from a shop-bought fruit's seeds?
faqs.a: Often, yes. Purple and Panama passionfruit grow readily from fresh seed scooped straight from a ripe fruit, and because the woodiness virus is not carried in seed, seedlings start out clean. They will not have a grafted vine's resistance to soil disease, and a named variety may not come perfectly true, but home-raised vines are cheap and can crop well. Sow fresh seed in a warm spot and keep it moist.

QLD.intro: Growing passionfruit in Queensland. This is the home of the Australian passionfruit: the state grows the great majority of the national crop, across a warm, frost-free run of country from the subtropical south-east up the tropical coast.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm, humid, largely frost-free climate is close to ideal for passionfruit, which is why the state produces most of Australia's crop. The subtropical south-east suits the purple and black types that most home gardeners want, while the hot, humid tropical coast favours the larger Panama and golden types that shrug off heat. Only the cold, high inland is genuinely marginal.

sections.heading: Where passionfruit grows in Queensland
sections.body: The main districts run from the Sunshine Coast and its hinterland through the Wide Bay and on up the tropical coast as far as Cooktown and the Daintree. Backyard vines crop happily across Brisbane and the south-east, and commercial plantings follow the warm coast north. As a rule, grow purple types in the cooler south-east and Panama or golden types in the humid far north.

sections.heading: Harvest window in Queensland
sections.body: Queensland picks passionfruit across much of the year, with the fruit most plentiful through summer and autumn and the Panama types often starting a little earlier. The purple vines drop their ripe fruit to be gathered from the ground, so in the warm months growers clear the ground under the vines twice a day before the sun marks the fruit.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Queensland
sections.body: Queensland's warmth and summer rain bring the heaviest disease pressure in the country. The leaf and fruit spots, brown spot (Alternaria) above all, are the top problem in warm wet spells, with anthracnose close behind. Queensland fruit fly is the key insect: the female stings the maturing fruit, so baiting, trapping and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit all matter. Passionfruit woodiness virus, fusarium wilt and phytophthora root and collar rot are present as well, which is part of why vines here are replaced often.

faqs.q: Which passionfruit suits humid, tropical North Queensland?
faqs.a: In the hot, humid tropics the Panama and golden types, such as Panama Red and Panama Gold, are the dependable choice, because they take the heat and humidity far better than the purple types, which sulk and disease more readily up north. Keep the purple and black varieties for the cooler, drier south-east of the state.

faqs.q: How do I keep fruit fly out of my passionfruit?
faqs.a: Manage it actively. Hang traps to monitor numbers, use protein baits or registered cover sprays as the fruit develops, and pick up and bin or solarise any fallen or stung fruit promptly so the maggots cannot complete their life cycle. Netting a small vine works too, and staying on top of fallen fruit helps with disease as well.

NSW.intro: Growing passionfruit in New South Wales. The warm north coast is genuine passionfruit country, while frost sets the limit as you move south and inland, so choosing the right site matters more here than in Queensland.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Passionfruit thrives along the warm, subtropical north coast of New South Wales, where the purple and black types crop reliably. Frost is the limiting factor elsewhere: the cold Northern Tablelands and the inland slopes and plains are marginal, and even around Sydney and on the Central Coast you want a warm, sheltered, sunny position out of cold hollows.

sections.heading: Where passionfruit grows in New South Wales
sections.body: The reliable belt is the far north coast near the Queensland border, around the Tweed and down through Coffs Harbour and the Mid North Coast, where the climate is closest to subtropical Queensland. Sheltered gardens around Sydney grow it well too. The colder Northern Tablelands, the Riverina and the western inland are too frost-prone for a dependable crop without protection.

sections.heading: Harvest window in New South Wales
sections.body: On the New South Wales coast passionfruit crops in two main flushes, the larger over summer and into autumn and a lighter one later in the year. Because the coast is a touch cooler than Queensland, fruit tends to ripen a little later, and the season is shorter the further south and the higher up you go.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in New South Wales
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the main insect pest right through the passionfruit-growing parts of New South Wales, stinging the fruit, so monitor and bait for it. The passionvine hopper is a common sap-sucker here: it does little direct harm but coats the vine in sticky honeydew that grows sooty mould. Passionfruit woodiness virus, and in wet coastal summers brown spot, also turn up, so keep vines healthy and replace tired ones.

faqs.q: Will a vine survive frost in Sydney and the cold inland?
faqs.a: Young vines are frost-tender and a hard frost can kill them, so in Sydney's cooler suburbs, the tablelands and the inland, plant against a warm wall or fence, choose a hardy grafted vine, and protect new vines through their first winters. In genuinely cold districts, treat passionfruit as a short-lived gamble rather than a permanent fixture.

faqs.q: Which variety is the safest pick for a cool coastal garden?
faqs.a: For the cooler coast the purple and black types, such as Sweetheart, Misty Gem and Pandora, give the best flavour and the steadiest crops, and a grafted vine adds vigour and disease resistance. Save the Panama and golden types for the warmest far-northern gardens, where their heat tolerance is more use than their larger size.

WA.intro: Growing passionfruit in Western Australia. Passionfruit grows well on Perth's frost-free coastal plain and has been a backyard favourite in the south-west since the 1920s. What sets WA apart is its different fruit fly and a strict quarantine that shapes where you can buy a vine.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The Perth coastal plain and the wider south-west are largely frost-free and grow passionfruit well; the purple types have been grown commercially here from Albany to Perth since the 1920s and remain a common garden plant. There is also a small passionfruit industry up the warm west coast and around Kununurra in the tropical north. The cold southern hills and the dry interior are more marginal, where frost and exposure cut the vine back.

sections.heading: Where passionfruit grows in Western Australia
sections.body: The dependable areas are the Perth sandplain and the south-west fruit belt running from around Gingin down toward Manjimup, plus the warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon and the tropical Ord around Kununurra in the far north. On Perth's deep sands passionfruit grows fast, but needs feeding and watering to match.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Western Australia
sections.body: Western Australia has Mediterranean fruit fly rather than the Queensland fruit fly of the eastern states. Medfly is established from Esperance to Carnarvon, is a declared pest, and will use passionfruit among its many host fruits, so baiting and trapping are part of growing here. On the upside, WA's drier summers ease the fungal leaf spots that plague the humid east, though passionfruit woodiness virus still shortens vines here as it does everywhere.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to Western Australia
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine shapes how you buy. Live plants from other states must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and are usually treated and certified before they can enter, so many eastern nurseries simply will not post vines here. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA. Buying from a local Western Australian nursery, or raising a vine from seed saved from local fruit, sidesteps the import hurdle entirely.

faqs.q: Do passionfruit need a lot of water on Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes. Perth's deep sands drain very fast and hold little, so a passionfruit here needs deep, regular watering through the dry summer and a thick, renewed mulch to keep the roots cool and damp. Dig compost in before planting, feed little and often because nutrients wash straight through sand, and use a wetting agent if the sand starts to repel water. Even so, never let the vine sit in a wet, poorly drained spot.

faqs.q: Which passionfruit is best for a Perth backyard?
faqs.a: In Perth's warm, frost-free climate almost any passionfruit does well, but for a backyard the purple and black types such as Sweetheart and Misty Gem give the best flavour, and a grafted vine copes better with soil disease in sandy ground. Leave the Panama and golden types for the hotter north of the state. Whichever you choose, buy from a Western Australian seller, since quarantine limits what can be posted in.

VIC.intro: Growing passionfruit in Victoria. Victoria is cool and frost-prone for a subtropical vine, so passionfruit here is a crop for the keen gardener, grown in the warmest corner of the garden and not expected to last long.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria sits at the cold edge of passionfruit country. The vine is frost-tender and the warm season is short, so a passionfruit will not crop the way it does up north and is best treated as a short-lived, for-fun plant rather than a reliable one. It wants the warmest, most sheltered microclimate you can give it, ideally against a sun-baked north-facing wall.

sections.heading: Grafted vines for a cool climate
sections.body: In Victoria the grafted Nellie Kelly passionfruit is the dependable pick. It is worked onto a hardy blue passionflower (Passiflora caerulea) rootstock that adds cold tolerance and vigour, which is just what a Melbourne garden needs. The catch is that same rootstock: rub off any shoots that appear from below the graft or up out of the ground, because the blue passionflower is a vigorous spreader and will take over and give poor fruit if you let it.

sections.heading: Planting and frost protection
sections.body: Plant in spring so the vine has the whole warm season to establish before its first winter. Give it a hot, sheltered, north-facing spot, and protect young vines from frost with cloth or a temporary cover through their first couple of winters. Expect the fruit to ripen slowly and the season to be shorter than in the subtropics; in the coldest districts a vine may grow well yet set little fruit.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Victoria
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly has spread into Victoria and will sting passionfruit, so it now needs managing here as well as in the warmer states. Cool, wet conditions are the other risk: poorly drained soil rots passionfruit roots, so sharp drainage and a raised or mounded bed matter. As everywhere, passionfruit woodiness virus shortens a vine's life, so replace one that turns sickly rather than nursing it along.

faqs.q: Will passionfruit actually fruit in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Yes, with the right vine and spot. A grafted Nellie Kelly against a hot, sheltered wall will crop in Melbourne and the milder coastal districts like the Mornington Peninsula, though more lightly and later than in the subtropics. In the coldest inland and hill districts it is a gamble: the vine may grow well but set little fruit, and frost can cut it back, so treat it as a short-lived novelty there.

faqs.q: Why is my grafted passionfruit suddenly bearing small, hard, inedible fruit?
faqs.a: The likeliest cause is that the rootstock has taken over. If the top of a grafted vine dies back or is cut down by frost, the blue passionflower rootstock shoots away from below and replaces it, and its fruit is small, hard and not worth eating. Trace the stems down: if they are rising from below the graft, the variety you bought is gone, and it is time to pull it out and replant.
